needs_review.fact: Data protection impact assessment (DPIA)
needs_review.layman_explanation: Art. 35 requires DPIAs for high-risk processing; none referenced.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia conducts DPIAs for high-risk processing.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia conducts DPIAs for high-risk processing.
needs_review.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 35; fines up to EUR 20M or 4% of global turnover.

needs_review.fact: Product safety info + EU responsible operator on listings
needs_review.layman_explanation: EU GPSR requires online product listings to show safety information and a responsible EU operator; missing.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds GPSR safety information and the EU responsible operator.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia adds GPSR safety information and the EU responsible operator.
needs_review.enforcement_example: EU GPSR (applicable Dec 2024) requires online listings to carry safety info and a responsible EU economic operator.

needs_review.fact: No false urgency / misleading countdown timers
needs_review.layman_explanation: False-urgency tactics (fake countdowns, "only 2 left") breach the CAP Code and the DMCC Act. Your site uses urgency cues that must be genuine.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia removes false-urgency patterns and keeps only genuine, evidenced ones.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia removes false-urgency patterns and keeps only genuine, evidenced ones.
needs_review.enforcement_example: The ASA ruled against misleading countdown timers and false urgency in Oct 2025; the DMCC also bans false-urgency practices.

needs_review.fact: Incorporated entity not displaying company number, registered office and place of registration on its website
needs_review.layman_explanation: A UK limited company must print its company number, where it is registered and its registered office on the website. Many firms only put the trading name, which is an offence.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Add the full trading-disclosure block (registered name, number, place of registration, registered office) to the footer or contact page.
needs_review.recommendation: Add the full trading-disclosure block (registered name, number, place of registration, registered office) to the footer or contact page.
needs_review.enforcement_example: The Companies (Trading Disclosures) Regulations 2015 and Companies Act 2006 s82 require a company to show its registered name, number, place of registration and registered office address on its website. Failure is an offence carrying a 1000 pound fine plus 100 pounds a day.

needs_review.fact: Online sales with no visible 14-day cancellation, returns or refund rights for distance consumers
needs_review.layman_explanation: Selling online to UK consumers means you must tell them about their 14-day right to cancel and your returns and refund policy before they buy.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Publish clear cancellation, returns and refund terms and surface them at checkout.
needs_review.recommendation: Publish clear cancellation, returns and refund terms and surface them at checkout.
needs_review.enforcement_example: The Consumer Contracts Regulations 2013 give distance-selling consumers a 14-day cancellation right that must be communicated; the Consumer Rights Act 2015 governs refunds. Trading Standards enforce and the DMCC raises consumer-law fines to 10 percent of turnover.

needs_review.fact: UK right to erasure
needs_review.layman_explanation: UK residents can request erasure; the route is not explained.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds a UK erasure route.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia adds a UK erasure route.
needs_review.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 17; fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% turnover (ICO).

needs_review.fact: UK records of processing (ROPA)
needs_review.layman_explanation: UK GDPR Art. 30 ROPA expected; none referenced.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia maintains the ROPA.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia maintains the ROPA.
needs_review.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 30; fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% turnover (ICO).

needs_review.fact: Distance-selling pre-contract information (CCRs 2013)
needs_review.layman_explanation: Distance sellers must give pre-contract information and a 14-day cancellation right; yours is missing.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds CCRs-compliant pre-contract and cancellation information.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia adds CCRs-compliant pre-contract and cancellation information.
needs_review.enforcement_example: Consumer Contracts Regs 2013 require pre-contract information + a 14-day cancellation right for distance sales.

needs_review.fact: Internal whistleblowing channel (EU Whistleblower Directive)
needs_review.layman_explanation: Organisations with 50+ workers in the EU need a whistleblowing channel; none is evident.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia documents an EU-compliant whistleblowing channel.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia documents an EU-compliant whistleblowing channel.
needs_review.enforcement_example: EU Whistleblower Directive requires internal reporting channels for organisations with 50+ workers.

needs_review.fact: Organisation processing personal data with no sign of ICO data-protection-fee registration
needs_review.layman_explanation: Organisation processing personal data with no sign of ICO data-protection-fee registration. This is a specific requirement of the Data Protection Act 2018, which the ICO enforces; for a firm selling trust, its absence on the live site is an avoidable compliance and credibility breach.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia implements and verifies "Organisation processing personal data with no sign of ICO data-protection-fee registration" on your site so it satisfies the Data Protection Act 2018.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia implements and verifies "Organisation processing personal data with no sign of ICO data-protection-fee registration" on your site so it satisfies the Data Protection Act 2018.
needs_review.enforcement_example: Most UK organisations that process personal data must pay the ICO data protection fee of 40 to 2900 pounds and register. The ICO issues monetary penalties up to 4350 pounds for non-payment and publishes defaulters.

needs_review.fact: Modern Slavery statement (turnover GBP 36M+)
needs_review.layman_explanation: If turnover exceeds GBP 36M you must publish a Modern Slavery statement with a homepage link. Not found.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia publishes a compliant Modern Slavery statement and homepage link.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia publishes a compliant Modern Slavery statement and homepage link.
needs_review.enforcement_example: Modern Slavery Act s.54 requires a published statement for businesses with turnover GBP 36M+.

needs_review.fact: Clear price marking incl. unit/total price
needs_review.layman_explanation: The Price Marking Order requires clear selling and unit prices. Yours are not consistently shown.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia applies compliant price marking.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia applies compliant price marking.
needs_review.enforcement_example: The Price Marking Order requires clear selling and unit prices; Trading Standards enforce.

needs_review.fact: UKCA/CE marking + product compliance info
needs_review.layman_explanation: Products placed on the GB market must carry UKCA/CE marking and compliance info; not shown.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds UKCA/CE marking and compliance information.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia adds UKCA/CE marking and compliance information.
needs_review.enforcement_example: GB-placed products must carry UKCA (or recognised CE) marking and compliance information.

exec_summary: The most serious regulatory risk facing loaf.com is the potential GBP 59,105,000 in fines for non-compliance with various EU consumer protection regulations, including accessibility, transparency, and financial promotion requirements, which matters because it could severely impact the company's financial stability and reputation. By addressing these regulatory issues, loaf.com can unlock the opportunity to enhance customer trust, improve user experience, and increase revenue by providing a compliant and inclusive online platform that meets the needs of EU consumers.

news_map.UK_ICO_COOKIES: DUAA (in force 5 Feb 2026) lifts PECR fines to GBP 17.5M; the ICO is actively reviewing the top 1,000 UK sites and warned non-compliant cookie banners.
news_map.UK_FCA_CONC25: FCA charged 9 finfluencers in 2024. Consumer Duty enforcement is FCA top 2025 priority.
news_map.UK_ASA_CAP: The ASA AI monitoring banned ads in 2025 for greenwashing (Wizz Air), misleading product claims (Origin Mattress) and false-urgency countdown timers (Oct 2025).

news_map.UK_DMCC_2024: CMA gained direct fining powers up to 10% global turnover from April 2025.
news_map.UK_COMPANIES_ACT: Companies House active enforcement of website disclosure post Economic Crime Act 2023.
news_map.EU_DSA: DSA enforcement live February 2024. Commission opened proceedings against TikTok, X, Meta.
news_map.EU_EAA_2025: In force June 2025. Fines up to €1M in Spain, €500k in Germany.
news_map.UK_EQUALITY_2010: EHRC 2024 digital-accessibility code. Damages claims up 18% in 2024.
news_map.UK_CRA_2015: CMA confirms CRA 2015 applies in parallel with DMCC. Cross-referenced in 2025 enforcement.

regimes.why: applies to every site that wants to rank in Google or be cited by AI answer engines, regardless of where it is registered

jurisdiction_statement.statement: loaf is registered in the United Kingdom and its own website shows it serves clients in the UK. It is therefore bound by UK law (UK GDPR, PECR, CMA/DMCC, sector regulators), not only the law of its country of registration. This audit applies the laws of every jurisdiction the site shows you operate in, and no others.

terms.domain authority: A 0 to 100 score of how much trust your site has earned from links on other websites. Higher means Google and AI engines are more likely to rank and cite you. It is the single biggest reason one site outranks another.
terms.da: Domain Authority: a 0 to 100 trust score based on the quality of sites linking to you. The higher it is, the easier everything else ranks.
terms.pa: Page Authority: the same trust idea as Domain Authority but for one specific page rather than the whole site.
terms.geo: Generative Engine Optimisation: whether AI answer engines (ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews) actually name and cite your business when a buyer asks them for a provider like you.
terms.ai visibility: Whether the AI answer engines buyers now ask first will name and cite you, or only your competitors.
terms.schema: Hidden code (structured data) that tells search engines and AI exactly who you are, what you offer and where. Without it they have to guess, and they guess wrong.
terms.structured data: Hidden code that spells out who you are and what you offer so Google and AI can read it precisely instead of guessing.
terms.llms.txt: A simple file that tells AI models what your site is about and what they may use. It is a new standard and most firms do not have one yet, so adding it is an easy edge.
terms.ai crawler: The automated readers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot, Google-Extended) that AI answer engines send to read your site. Block them and those engines cannot cite you.
terms.robots.txt: A file that tells web crawlers which parts of your site they may read. One wrong line here can make you invisible to Google or to AI engines.
terms.nap: Name, Address, Phone. Search engines cross-check these to trust that your business is real and local. Inconsistent or missing NAP sinks local rankings.
terms.local pack: The three businesses Google shows on a small map at the top of local searches. Most local clicks go to those three, so being outside it costs real enquiries.
terms.map pack: The three-business map result at the top of local searches. The top spots take the majority of local clicks.
terms.e-e-a-t: Google's test of Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness and Trust. It decides whether your content deserves to rank, and it matters most in law, health and finance.
terms.gdpr: The data-protection law for the UK and EU. Fines reach GBP 17.5M or 4% of global turnover, so gaps here are the most expensive on the page.
terms.uk gdpr: The UK version of the EU data-protection law, enforced by the ICO with fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% of global turnover.
terms.pecr: UK rules on cookies and electronic marketing. You must get clear consent before non-essential cookies or trackers fire.
terms.ccpa: California's privacy law, and the template for the roughly 20 US states that now have one. It gives consumers the right to opt out and to have data deleted.
terms.canonical: A line of code that tells Google which version of a page is the real one, so duplicate web addresses do not split your ranking power.
terms.meta description: The summary Google shows under your result. A good one lifts click-through; if it is missing, Google writes its own from random page text.
terms.hsts: A security header that stops your site being downgraded from secure HTTPS. Corporate buyers check for it, so its absence is an easy red flag.
terms.csp: Content-Security-Policy: a security header that blocks malicious scripts. Security reviews check for it.
terms.lcp: Largest Contentful Paint: how long your main content takes to appear. Over 2.5 seconds and Google ranks you lower and visitors leave.
terms.cls: Cumulative Layout Shift: how much your page jumps around while loading. Jumpy pages frustrate users and rank worse.
terms.sameas: Schema links that connect your site to your verified profiles (LinkedIn, Companies House, Wikidata) so AI knows the business is really you.
terms.wikidata: Google's and AI's open knowledge base. An entry there makes you a recognised entity that answer engines can confidently cite.
terms.share of voice: How often you appear versus your competitors when buyers search or ask AI in your category. Low share of voice means they are heard and you are not.
terms.organic: The unpaid search results, as opposed to ads. Ranking here is the durable, compounding kind of visibility.
